text: The laparoscopic repair begins with the use of a flange retractor to keep the bowels away in an obese patient. The surgeon performs a subtotal hysterectomy, followed by dissection of the uterovascular fold and opening of the anterior space between the vagina and bladder. The dissection proceeds to the bladder trigone, facilitated by pulling the bladder upwards and using the cavitational effect of carbon dioxide. A three-dimensional camera aids in visualizing the deep dissection.

The surgeon ligates and anchors the pre-cut mesh in the anterior space, using two anchor stitches along the vaginal wall at the junction between the vagina, cervix, and bladder. Esterless three-zero non-absorbable sutures are used for anchoring. Additional anchor stitches are placed lower to stabilize the mesh, followed by two more stitches at the apex of the cervix or pubocervical ligament to ensure complete mesh anchoring.

The surgeon uses a needle holder to pull the long arms of the mesh out of the abdomen extraperitoneally, positioning them just outside the abdomen with artery forceps. The mesh is then extraperitonealized to prevent exposure to the peritoneal cavity contents, completing the surgery.
